description: The follow-on plan explicitly references prior named-profile and Teams EE-boundary work so this migration is scoped as a continuation rather than a rewrite.
The plan treats calendar EE migration and Microsoft explicit-binding cleanup as one coordinated effort with one shared acceptance checklist.
Shared Microsoft profiles remain shared infrastructure instead of being duplicated into an EE-only credential model.
Calendar sync is modeled as one EE feature rather than a collection of partially shared settings and runtime behaviors.
Microsoft consumers are modeled as explicit bindings instead of compatibility defaults or implicit cross-consumer fallbacks.
CE ownership is limited to stubs, wrappers, or delegators where a route or import boundary must remain addressable.
The migration does not add new product scope beyond EE ownership cleanup and Microsoft binding cleanup.
The plan does not preserve legacy compatibility wording or pane structure when it conflicts with the explicit-binding target model.
A CE availability contract is defined for every calendar settings, profile, route, and runtime surface.
An EE availability contract is defined for every calendar settings, profile, route, and runtime surface.
A CE availability contract is defined for Microsoft profile UI that should expose only MSP SSO.
An EE availability contract is defined for Microsoft profile UI that should expose MSP SSO plus additional supported consumers.
Shared modules are required to consume edition-safe calendar entrypoints instead of concrete shared calendar implementations.
Shared modules are required to consume edition-safe Microsoft consumer helpers instead of compatibility default resolution.
One source of truth exists for deciding whether a calendar surface is active, stubbed, or unavailable by edition.
One source of truth exists for deciding which Microsoft consumer options are visible by edition.
The migration inventory identifies every settings, profile, route, action, service, and subscriber file touched by the calendar EE move.
The migration inventory identifies every settings, action, and compatibility surface touched by the Microsoft binding cleanup.
The PRD documents which compatibility behaviors are intentionally deleted rather than preserved.
The PRD documents which routes must stay stable even though ownership moves to EE.
The plan documents the CE stub response shape for calendar settings and callback surfaces.
The plan documents the EE delegation pattern for calendar settings and callback surfaces.
The plan documents the CE-versus-EE Microsoft profile surface matrix.
The plan documents the final acceptance matrix for edition ownership, bindings, and regression coverage.
The Integrations settings page no longer shows a Calendar category in CE.
The Integrations settings page continues to show a Calendar category in EE.
The shared Integrations settings composition stops rendering a live calendar configuration component in CE.
The shared Integrations settings composition renders calendar through an EE entrypoint or stubbed wrapper pattern.
The Calendar category description and copy are updated to match the EE-only ownership model.
The Calendar settings component is re-homed behind an enterprise entry component rather than a shared direct import.
The Calendar settings component follows the same lazy-load or modular entry pattern used by other EE settings surfaces where appropriate.
CE requests that specify the Calendar category fall back safely to an available category instead of leaving a broken tab state.
The selected-category URL handling tolerates the absence of Calendar in CE without client-side errors.
No duplicate Calendar entry remains under Communication, Providers, or other shared settings categories.
Shared provider settings no longer link CE users toward Calendar setup flows.
EE settings can still deep-link into Calendar setup from consumer-owned surfaces where needed.
The user profile page no longer renders a Calendar tab in CE.
The user profile page continues to render a Calendar tab in EE.
CE profile tab routing ignores a Calendar tab query parameter safely and falls back to a valid tab.
EE profile tab routing preserves and restores the Calendar tab state.
Calendar settings unavailable states use explicit EE-style messaging only when a route or wrapper must remain addressable in CE.
Calendar settings do not remain discoverable in CE through hidden cards, disabled shells, or stale navigation items.
Calendar settings continue to present the real configuration UX in EE rather than a placeholder shell.
The profile page does not expose a dead-end or nonfunctional Calendar tab in CE.
Settings and profile tests cover edition-specific visibility for calendar entrypoints.
Documentation and screenshots stop describing Calendar as a shared integration surface.
Runbooks and references for Calendar settings are updated to point to EE-owned entrypoints.
The overall settings information architecture remains internally consistent after the Calendar category becomes EE-only.
The Google calendar OAuth callback route becomes a CE stub or EE delegator rather than a shared active implementation.
The Microsoft calendar OAuth callback route becomes a CE stub or EE delegator rather than a shared active implementation.
CE requests to the Google calendar callback route return an EE-unavailable response and do not exchange tokens.
CE requests to the Microsoft calendar callback route return an EE-unavailable response and do not exchange tokens.
EE requests to the Google calendar callback route delegate to an EE-owned implementation.
EE requests to the Microsoft calendar callback route delegate to an EE-owned implementation.
Shared callback wrapper files stop importing concrete calendar provider services and adapters directly.
EE callback implementations own provider creation, token exchange, and persistence side effects.
Calendar-specific OAuth state validation remains intact after the callback routes move behind EE wrappers.
The existing calendar callback success contract remains stable for EE tenants after the refactor.
The existing calendar callback error contract remains stable for EE tenants after the refactor.
Direct CE access to calendar callback routes cannot create calendar providers, provider tokens, or sync mappings.
Edition-aware callback tests verify gating before any provider write occurs.
Calendar callback wrappers preserve request-method handling and expected route semantics during EE delegation.
Calendar callback wrappers preserve query parsing and state extraction semantics during EE delegation.
Contract tests assert that active calendar callback logic no longer lives in shared CE runtime modules.
Any calendar webhook or subscription-maintenance entry routes become CE stubs or EE delegators.
CE webhook or maintenance entry routes reject or no-op cleanly without running calendar logic.
EE webhook or maintenance entry routes delegate to EE-owned implementations.
Shared route registries stop advertising live calendar endpoints as shared CE capabilities.
Middleware and auth handling no longer treat calendar callback routes as a shared CE feature surface.
Route-level tests cover CE unavailable behavior, EE active behavior, and malformed calendar callback input.
Route runbooks are updated to document EE ownership for calendar callbacks and maintenance entrypoints.
The public callback URLs remain stable for EE tenants even though the implementation ownership changes.
The shared Calendar integrations UI module is replaced by an EE-owned concrete implementation behind a stable entry boundary.
The shared Calendar provider service no longer owns live calendar provider CRUD behavior in CE.
The shared Calendar sync service no longer owns live calendar synchronization behavior in CE.
The shared Google Calendar adapter no longer provides live CE runtime behavior.
The shared Microsoft Calendar adapter no longer provides live CE runtime behavior.
The shared calendar webhook maintenance service no longer provides live CE runtime behavior.
Calendar provider configuration create, update, and delete behavior executes only in EE-owned runtime code.
Calendar connect and disconnect behavior executes only in EE-owned runtime code.
Calendar sync conflict publication and handling execute only in EE-owned runtime code.
Calendar external-event import and merge logic execute only in EE-owned runtime code.
Calendar provider status updates and error-state transitions execute only in EE-owned runtime code.
Calendar mapping persistence and repair logic remain reachable only through EE-owned runtime code.
CE startup no longer registers calendar subscribers or background sync handlers.
EE startup continues to register calendar subscribers or background sync handlers.
Schedule entry events in CE no longer trigger calendar synchronization side effects.
Schedule entry events in EE continue to trigger calendar synchronization side effects.
Shared packages expose only abstract, stubbed, or delegated calendar entrypoints after the extraction.
Enterprise packages expose the concrete calendar runtime modules after the extraction.
Ownership tests assert that shared packages do not reach into EE calendar code through raw filesystem-relative imports.
Ownership tests assert that CE builds can import calendar wrappers without loading active calendar logic.
The calendar EE ownership contract explicitly includes webhook-renewal job paths.
The calendar EE ownership contract explicitly includes provider secret handling paths.
The calendar EE ownership contract explicitly includes adapter-selection and provider-type branching paths.
The calendar EE ownership contract explicitly includes any remaining background maintenance and subscription-renewal paths.
The Microsoft profile page in CE describes Microsoft profile reuse only in MSP SSO terms.
The Microsoft profile page in EE describes Microsoft profile reuse for MSP SSO plus additional supported consumers.
CE Microsoft profile UI omits Email consumer guidance.
CE Microsoft profile UI omits Calendar consumer guidance.
CE Microsoft profile UI omits Teams consumer guidance.
CE Microsoft profile UI continues to show MSP SSO guidance and login-domain-related support copy.
EE Microsoft profile UI shows MSP SSO consumer guidance.
EE Microsoft profile UI shows Email consumer guidance.
EE Microsoft profile UI shows Calendar consumer guidance.
EE Microsoft profile UI shows Teams consumer guidance.
CE Microsoft profile UI omits non-SSO redirect URI guidance blocks.
EE Microsoft profile UI renders redirect URI guidance for supported non-SSO consumers.
CE Microsoft profile UI omits Teams Application ID URI guidance.
EE Microsoft profile UI renders Teams Application ID URI guidance when relevant metadata is available.
Create and edit profile dialogs stop referring to a default compatibility profile in CE.
Create and edit profile dialogs stop referring to a default compatibility profile in EE.
CE profile cards show only MSP SSO-related binding state and readiness context.
EE profile cards show binding state and readiness context for MSP SSO plus the supported EE consumers.
CE readiness copy references only MSP SSO-related prerequisites and usage.
EE readiness copy references the broader set of supported Microsoft consumers.
CE empty-state copy refers only to MSP SSO-oriented profile creation and reuse.
EE empty-state copy refers to broader Microsoft profile reuse across supported consumers.
Success toasts and helper copy stop describing Microsoft profiles as a compatibility source for legacy consumers.
The legacy Microsoft consumers pane is removed from the Microsoft profile page in all editions.
An explicit binding control exists for the MSP SSO consumer.
An explicit binding control exists for the Email consumer in EE.
An explicit binding control exists for the Calendar consumer in EE.
An explicit binding control exists for the Teams consumer in EE.
Binding UX is presented as one explicit row or control per consumer instead of a legacy consumers summary pane.
Binding UX shows the currently selected Microsoft profile for each visible consumer.
Archived Microsoft profiles are excluded from binding selection choices.
Binding UX surfaces archived-profile or readiness warnings when a currently bound profile is no longer healthy.
Changing one consumer binding does not modify unrelated consumer bindings.
Consumer binding changes are always tenant-scoped.
Consumer binding changes require the same system settings update permission used for Microsoft profile management.
Client-portal users cannot manage Microsoft consumer bindings.
CE exposes only the MSP SSO binding control.
CE does not render an Email binding control.
CE does not render a Calendar binding control.
CE does not render a Teams binding control.
EE renders all supported consumer binding controls consistently in the Microsoft profile management experience or its EE-owned extensions.
Binding UX supports clearing or reassigning a consumer only where the product rules explicitly allow it.
Teams rebinding continues to invalidate stale Teams install state without mutating unrelated bindings.
Calendar rebinding invalidates stale calendar provider configuration or prompts reconnection where required by the implementation.
Email rebinding invalidates or warns about stale Outlook provider connections where required by the implementation.
Binding UX copy explains that bindings are the source of truth for consumer selection.
Binding UX no longer relies on the old Current consumers badge block to communicate compatibility ownership.
Binding tests cover create, reassign, archived guard, permission guard, and edition visibility.
The consumer binding table becomes the only active source of consumer selection for migrated Microsoft consumers.
Legacy default fallback for MSP SSO is removed after explicit binding rollout is complete.
Legacy default fallback for Calendar is removed.
Teams remains an explicit-binding-only consumer.
Any retained Email fallback behavior is isolated as a temporary migration concern rather than the target design.
Legacy compatibility backfill routines are deleted or reduced to one-time migration-only responsibilities rather than steady-state runtime behavior.
The binding list action returns an edition-aware set of visible consumers.
CE binding-list responses include only MSP SSO.
EE binding-list responses include MSP SSO plus supported EE consumers.
The binding write action enforces edition-aware allowed consumer types.
CE binding-write requests for Email are rejected.
CE binding-write requests for Calendar are rejected.
CE binding-write requests for Teams are rejected.
EE binding-write requests for supported consumers succeed when inputs are valid.
Consumer resolution prefers explicit binding rows before any legacy fallback logic is considered.
Consumer resolution returns no profile for migrated consumers when no explicit binding exists and fallback has been removed.
MSP SSO resolution uses the explicit binding path.
Email integration resolution uses the explicit binding path wherever that consumer remains supported.
Calendar integration resolution uses the explicit binding path.
Teams integration resolution continues to use the explicit binding path.
Microsoft integration status payloads return edition-appropriate redirect URI and scope metadata.
CE Microsoft status payloads omit non-SSO consumer details that should not appear in CE.
EE Microsoft status payloads include the non-SSO consumer details needed by EE profile management.
Action-layer tests cover the edition matrix, missing-binding behavior, archive guards, and removal of compatibility-default assumptions.
The existing Microsoft consumer binding table remains shared infrastructure after the migration.
The supported consumer constraint set continues to represent MSP SSO, Email, Calendar, and Teams unless a deliberate narrowing migration is chosen.
Migration strategy backfills explicit MSP SSO bindings for existing tenants that rely on named Microsoft profiles.
Migration strategy defines how explicit Email bindings are created for any retained Outlook email behavior.
Migration strategy backfills explicit Calendar bindings for existing EE tenants with connected calendar providers where needed.
Migration strategy preserves explicit Teams binding data.
No runtime consumer-resolution path depends on default-profile compatibility semantics after the cutover target state is reached.
The default-profile concept is reduced to profile-management metadata only if it remains in schema at all.
UI copy and behavior no longer rely on `is_default` as a compatibility signal for consumer routing.
Shared migrations and comments stop describing consumer resolution in compatibility-default terms.
Calendar schema and runtime ownership are documented as enterprise-owned in the migration plan.
A fresh CE install exposes no executable calendar feature surface even when shared schema objects exist.
A fresh EE install exposes the calendar feature surface through EE-owned codepaths.
Existing calendar provider rows remain usable by EE after ownership moves behind EE boundaries.
CE access to existing calendar provider rows cannot execute calendar synchronization behavior.
Migration tests cover tenants with no Microsoft profiles.
Migration tests cover tenants with one default Microsoft profile and no binding rows.
Migration tests cover tenants with existing calendar providers that need explicit binding alignment.
Migration tests cover tenants with archived Microsoft profiles.
Migration tests cover invalid cross-tenant binding attempts.
Migration runbooks document any unsupported edge states that require manual cleanup instead of silent compatibility behavior.
Schema documentation reflects shared binding infrastructure plus EE calendar ownership.
Scratchpad notes record the migration files and ownership boundaries that implement the cutover.
Contract tests assert both the Microsoft binding schema contract and the EE ownership of calendar runtime paths.
The old legacy Microsoft consumers pane codepath is removed.
The old default-compatibility-profile copy is removed.
Old tests that asserted the legacy Microsoft consumers pane are removed or replaced.
Old tests that asserted CE calendar presence are removed or replaced.
This plan cross-references the prior Teams EE-boundary migration plan.
This plan cross-references the prior named Microsoft profile and consumer-binding work.
The scratchpad records the key files affected by the calendar settings, profile, route, service, and subscriber refactor.
The scratchpad records the key files affected by Microsoft profile UI, action, and binding cleanup.
The runbook includes the plan-validator command for this plan folder.
The runbook includes the focused unit and integration suite list expected to validate this migration.
A CE review checklist confirms no calendar UI or runtime remains in shared surfaces.
An EE review checklist confirms the calendar flow remains complete after extraction.
A CE review checklist confirms the Microsoft profile page shows only MSP SSO-oriented content.
An EE review checklist confirms the Microsoft profile page shows the additional supported consumers.
A regression checklist confirms the earlier Teams EE cleanup remains intact.
A regression checklist confirms MSP SSO discovery and login-domain flows remain intact.
A regression checklist confirms the chosen Email consumer strategy is intentionally covered rather than left to compatibility defaults.
A regression checklist confirms binding changes do not regress profile archive or delete guards.
A regression checklist confirms CE stubs fail clearly instead of partially executing calendar logic.
A regression checklist confirms EE wrappers delegate to enterprise implementations instead of stale shared code.
The final acceptance checklist ties together the calendar EE move and explicit-binding cleanup as one cohesive migration.
The implementation plan stays self-contained under one dated plan folder with synchronized PRD, features, tests, and scratchpad artifacts.
Plan validation passes for artifact schema, feature/test shape, and cross-reference integrity.
The plan inventory remains comprehensive enough to drive a multi-commit migration without reopening scope discovery.
